instructions.whoFillsThisIn: Developers (Product weighs in on look & feel / user interface for website and application solutions).
instructions.when: Once per solution. This file sits at the root of the solution's folder and defines how the whole solution is built. Each item (page, endpoint, or database-model) is a separate file in the same folder.
instructions.how: Set solutionType first: website | api | database | application. It tells you which of the fields below apply — each one that isn't relevant to your type says so in its guidance; leave those blank. Answer the rest in plain English, then hand it to Claude using the bridge in claude-only/1-translate-to-claude.md.
instructions.note: Solution = the folder this file sits in. One form now covers every solution type, replacing what used to be separate Website-spec.json / API-spec.json / Database-spec.json / Application-spec.json files — every solution folder's spec is named application-spec.json, whatever its solutionType.

purpose.question: What is the purpose of this solution?

example.website: The storefront customers use to browse and buy clothes, and to manage their account.
example.api: The single API for all requests from the clothing website.
example.database: Stores every clothing-store data model — accounts, orders, products.
example.application: A desktop tool that lets a small team plan and track work without a server.

hosting.question: Where will it be hosted, deployed, or distributed?

example.website: Azure Static Web Apps, Vercel, Netlify.
example.api: Azure Function App, AWS Lambda, a container.
example.database: Firebase, Azure SQL, Azure Elastic Pool, Azure Cosmos DB — or 'embedded inside <solution>' if it isn't separately hosted.
example.application: A downloadable native executable; no server-side hosting.

language.question: What language will it use?
language.guidance: For a database solution, put the database engine here instead.
language.example: TypeScript, Go, C#, Rust — or PostgreSQL, SQLite, Turso for a database.

frameworks.question: What frameworks, libraries, or UI toolkit should it use?
frameworks.guidance: Not applicable to most database solutions — leave blank.

accessAndInterface.guidance: Who or what is allowed to use this solution, and how they interact with it. Each field says which solutionType it applies to — leave the rest blank.

authType.question: How does this solution authenticate or authorise callers/users?
authType.guidance: Website, API, application. Not applicable to most database solutions — leave blank.

example.website: JWT returned by the API, stored client-side; roles gate which pages are shown.
example.application: Local roles enforced in-app, no login — or the OS user account.

rolesOrClaims.question: Does it authorise by Claims or Roles?
rolesOrClaims.guidance: Website, API, application. The roles/claims vocabulary itself is listed in the Project Brief. Not applicable to most database solutions.

apisItTalksTo.question: Which API(s) does it talk to?
apisItTalksTo.guidance: Website solutions only — name the API solution(s) it calls.

lookAndFeel.question: What is the overall look and feel?

stylingOrToolkit.question: What styling approach, component library, or UI toolkit?
stylingOrToolkit.example: Tailwind, Material UI (website) — ratatui, egui, Tauri, iced (application).

interactionModel.question: How do people interact with it?
interactionModel.example: Typed commands, mouse drag-and-drop, or both.

conventions.guidance: Technical conventions. Each field says which solutionType it applies to — leave the rest blank.

basePath.question: What is the main URL path?

versionedFolders.question: Are endpoints organised into version-specific folders?

configLocation.question: Where does it read/write its own config and data on disk?
configLocation.example: The OS-appropriate app-data folder, overridable by an environment variable.

errorLogging.question: How do we log errors and issues?

documentation.question: How is this solution documented for its users or callers?

standardResponses.guidance: API solutions only — one entry per status code the whole API uses consistently. Leave empty otherwise.

scaffold.guidance: How this solution's repo is laid out and driven, so the AI can create the skeleton on the first build and verify its work on every build. Pick a boilerplate from _forms/boilerplates.json by name, or fill the fields in yourself — anything you fill in overrides the boilerplate's version of it.

boilerplate.question: Start from a named boilerplate?
boilerplate.example: react-vite, go-azure-functions, dotnet-webapi, sql-server-database-project, turso-embedded, rust-cli-app.

fileLayout.question: What is the folder and file layout of this solution's repo?
fileLayout.guidance: One entry per folder or key file, with what belongs in it.

testing.question: What test framework should be used, and what kinds of tests?

commands.question: The commands the AI should use to work with this repo.
commands.guidance: Fill in whichever apply — a database solution might only need build/test/deploy; a website/API/application typically needs install/run/build/test too.

security.guidance: The security rules this solution must obey on every build. Pick a named baseline from _forms/boilerplates.json (securityBaselines) and add any extra rules — extra rules are additions and can never weaken the baseline. The AI treats every rule here like a test: a build that breaks one is not done.

baseline.question: Start from a named security baseline?

rules.question: Any extra security rules for this solution?
rules.guidance: One entry per rule, in plain English.

infrastructure.guidance: What this solution runs on (or needs to build/sign/distribute), so the AI can create missing infrastructure and a pipeline deliberately — always as its own approved plan, never as a side effect of building an item. SECRETS ARE NEVER WRITTEN INTO CODE OR COMMITTED CONFIG: list every setting by name and where its value lives; the AI only ever references them by name.

resources.question: What infrastructure resources does this solution need?
resources.guidance: One entry per resource. Mark whether it already exists or the AI should create it. Leave empty if none.

provisioning.question: How is infrastructure created, and by whom?
provisioning.example: Bicep files in infra/, deployed by the pipeline — or 'a person creates resources in the portal; the AI never provisions' — or 'N/A, no infrastructure'.

environments.question: What environments exist, and which may the AI deploy to?
environments.example: dev (AI may deploy), production (people deploy after review).

settings.question: What named settings and secrets does this solution need at build or run time?
settings.guidance: Names and where each value lives — NEVER the values themselves.

exampleEntry.name: SQL_CONNECTION_STRING
exampleEntry.where: Azure Key Vault, referenced from application settings

pipeline.question: What CI/CD pipeline should this solution have?
pipeline.example: GitHub Actions: on PR → install, build, test; on merge to main → deploy to dev.

mustNotDo.question: Anything this solution must NOT do / cannot deploy?

limits.question: Any limits (rate limits, payload size, memory, startup time, supported platforms)?
